question: Was there a moment when you seriously questioned whether this subject was right for you? What happened, and what made you stay?
lookingFor: Honest acknowledgment of doubt alongside a reasoned recommitment — shows the motivation is tested, not just stated.

bullets: Name a specific moment of doubt, not a vague 'it was hard'
Explain what was genuinely difficult or unappealing in that moment
Show what brought you back and why that matters
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Halfway through a chemistry extended project I hit a wall where every experiment gave messy, inconclusive data, and I started wondering whether I was built for science at all. It was genuinely demotivating — not the exciting kind of hard. What brought me back was a conversation with my teacher who said unclear data is most of what real research looks like. That reframe changed everything. I realised I'd been expecting the subject to feel good all the time, and deciding to stay knowing it wouldn't was a different kind of commitment than the one I started with.

commonMistakes: Claiming there was never any doubt — sounds rehearsed and unconvincing
Describing doubt as purely external pressure rather than internal questioning
Staying but not explaining why — the reasoning is the whole answer
followUps: If that doubt came back during your degree, what would you do?

question: What did you give up — or what path did you not take — in order to pursue this subject? Do you have any regrets?
lookingFor: Evidence of a real trade-off consciously made, showing the applicant chose this path rather than defaulted to it.

bullets: Name a real alternative path or sacrifice, not something trivial
Show it was a considered choice, not just circumstance
Handle the regret question honestly — partial regret is fine and more credible
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I had a serious offer to pursue music professionally — I played piano to a competitive level and a conservatoire expressed interest. Choosing to apply for engineering instead meant closing that door. There's a small part of me that wonders how far I could have gone, and I think it's important to admit that. But every time I build something and it works, I know the decision was right for who I actually am. Music is still a huge part of my life; I just don't want it to be my livelihood.

commonMistakes: Saying you gave nothing up — either not true or suggests no real commitment
Framing the sacrifice so positively it sounds like no sacrifice at all
Expressing so much regret that the interviewer questions your choice
followUps: How does the path you didn't take still influence you?

question: What's something genuinely unappealing or problematic about this field that you've had to make peace with?
lookingFor: Mature, eyes-open motivation — the applicant knows the downsides and is choosing the field anyway.

bullets: Identify a real, substantive downside — not a surface-level inconvenience
Show you arrived at this through research or experience, not just assumption
Explain how you've genuinely reconciled with it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In publishing, which is where I want to work, the pay is notoriously low and entry routes can be opaque and depend heavily on connections. I found this out properly when I did a work-experience week at a literary agency and talked honestly with the junior staff. I've made peace with it partly because I know I'm going into it with open eyes, and partly because I've started building the relationships and the freelance track record that can give me more options. I'd rather know the landscape than be surprised by it.

commonMistakes: Naming a challenge that isn't actually a downside ('it's very competitive' — that's not 'unappealing')
Showing you haven't made peace with it at all — leaves the interviewer worried
Picking something so trivial it suggests you haven't really engaged with the field
followUps: Has learning that changed how you plan to enter the field?

question: Tell us about something in this subject you explored entirely on your own — nothing set by a teacher, no grade attached. What drew you to it?
lookingFor: Evidence of intrinsic curiosity — the applicant pursues the subject when no one is watching.

bullets: Name a specific topic, book, project, or rabbit hole with real detail
Explain what pulled you in — curiosity, a question, a link to something else
Show what you got out of it, even if it led nowhere formal
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent most of a summer reading about the history of zero — not just the maths, but the philosophy and the cultural resistance to the concept in different traditions. Nobody assigned it; I stumbled in through a footnote. What kept me was realising that a thing we treat as obvious required centuries of argument to accept, and that made me want to look at every concept I take for granted and ask whether it's actually settled. I don't think it changed my coursework directly, but it changed how I read everything in the subject.

commonMistakes: Citing something that was actually part of a class or extracurricular requirement
Describing an activity with no real engagement — 'I read a book' with no further detail
Making it sound impressive rather than genuinely curious
followUps: Where did that exploration lead? Did it open more questions than it answered?

question: Why this specific course structure, and not one of the more straightforward routes into this field?
lookingFor: Deliberate, informed reasoning about course design — not just 'I liked the campus'.

bullets: Show you understand what makes this course structurally different
Explain why that difference matters for how you learn or what you want to do
Be honest if part of the appeal is unconventional — that's fine
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I looked seriously at more traditional single-honours programmes, and the straightforward path felt like it would leave gaps I'd have to fill later anyway. This course's interdisciplinary core is unusual — most schools bolt on the second subject in the final year, but here it runs through from the start, which means the connections are baked in rather than grafted on. That's exactly how the problems I care about work in practice: they don't wait for you to finish your single subject first.

commonMistakes: Describing the course's features without explaining why they matter to you specifically
Showing you researched only this institution and no others — sounds lazy
Using prestige as the only differentiator
followUps: What would you do if the module you're most excited about turned out to be optional and oversubscribed?

question: You've told us about the experience that sparked your interest in this subject. But looking back honestly — how much of that story is something you've shaped in the telling, and how much was really decisive at the time?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty about how we construct our own motivational narratives — self-awareness under pressure.

bullets: Acknowledge that retrospective stories are always partly constructed — don't be defensive
Identify what was genuinely decisive versus what became meaningful in hindsight
Show this reflection doesn't undermine the motivation — just makes it more honest
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: That's fair to push on. The moment I usually describe — a particular lesson in Year 10 — probably wasn't as sharp at the time as it sounds now. What I can say is that the interest compounded: one thing led to an article, which led to a project, which led to a conversation that actually did feel decisive. The Year 10 lesson is probably shorthand for a slower accumulation. I think most honest origin stories are like that — we pick a starting point because it's easier to tell, not because it's the whole truth.

commonMistakes: Getting defensive and insisting the story is entirely genuine
Conceding so much that you seem to have no real motivation
Missing the point — this is about self-awareness, not about questioning your interest
followUps: So what's the truest version of why you're here?

question: What do you think you'll bring to your fellow students, not just to the academic work?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about non-academic contribution — intellectual culture, experience, perspective the cohort benefits from.

bullets: Name something specific and credible, not generic traits like 'enthusiasm'
Connect it to your background, experience, or how you actually engage with people
Show you've thought about the cohort as a community, not just as competition
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I come from a school where almost nobody went to university, so I've had to build a lot of context about this world independently, and I think that outsider perspective is actually useful in a group. I tend to ask questions that people who grew up assuming they'd get here might not think to ask — about how things actually work, who they're for, and whether the received wisdom holds. I also know from group work that I'm usually the person who keeps the argument alive when everyone wants to agree and move on, which isn't always comfortable but tends to produce better work.

commonMistakes: Generic claims: 'I'm hardworking and enthusiastic' — true of every applicant
Focusing entirely on what you'll get from the cohort, not give
Sounding like you'll be the most important person in the room
followUps: Is there a type of student or perspective you're hoping to encounter that's different from your own?

question: Who in this field do you most respect, and what specifically about their work or approach do you admire?
lookingFor: Field knowledge combined with personal values — the choice of role model reveals what the applicant cares about.

bullets: Name someone with real specificity — ideally not the most obvious household name
Describe something concrete about their work, not just their fame
Connect what you admire to something you're trying to build in yourself
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm drawn to Ursula Franklin's work — she was a materials scientist but spent most of her career arguing that technology always embeds values, and we have to ask whose. That framing completely reoriented how I think about what engineers are actually responsible for. What I admire isn't just the idea but the fact she kept making that argument in rooms that didn't always want to hear it. I want to bring that same refusal to separate the technical from the ethical.

commonMistakes: Naming the most famous person in the field without being able to say anything specific
Focusing on success rather than approach — 'they're very accomplished' tells us nothing
Choosing someone in a completely different field with a tenuous connection
followUps: What would that person make of where the field is heading right now?

question: What did you believe about this subject two years ago that you now think was wrong or incomplete?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty and growth — the applicant can articulate how their understanding has actually changed.

bullets: Name a specific belief or assumption, not just 'I knew less then'
Explain what changed your view — a book, an experience, a conversation
Show you're comfortable with the idea that your current view might also shift
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Two years ago I thought psychology was essentially applied common sense formalised — that experiments just confirmed things everyone already intuited. Reading about the replication crisis thoroughly dismantled that. A lot of famous findings don't hold up, and understanding why — publication bias, underpowered studies, incentive structures — made the subject much more interesting and much more difficult than I'd assumed. My view now is that the hard part of psychology isn't the findings, it's the methodology, and that's actually what I want to dig into.

commonMistakes: Describing a belief change that's trivial — just learning more facts isn't a changed belief
Being unable to name anything specific — suggests learning hasn't really happened
Showing the change so recently you haven't had time to examine it
followUps: What assumption about the subject do you think you might still be getting wrong?

question: How much of your interest in this subject is genuinely your own, and how much do you think has been shaped by the people around you — family, teachers, peers?
lookingFor: Honest self-examination of how the applicant arrived at their interest — awareness that external influence is normal, not disqualifying.

bullets: Acknowledge influence openly — trying to deny it reads as evasive
Distinguish between what was planted versus what you've made your own
Show the interest has a life of its own now, independent of the original source
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My father is an economist and I grew up around dinner-table arguments about policy, so it would be dishonest to say the subject appeared from nowhere. But I think there's a difference between being introduced to something and being shaped by it — and I've been going in directions he wouldn't choose for years now. The development economics angle I care about most he's fairly sceptical of, and yet I've read more on my own in that direction than anything a teacher assigned. At some point the interest becomes yours regardless of where it started.

commonMistakes: Insisting the interest is entirely self-generated — nobody arrives at a subject in a vacuum
Conceding so much influence that the interest seems secondhand
Getting defensive as if the question is an accusation
followUps: If your family had pushed you toward a different field entirely, where do you think you'd be now?

question: What's the last thing you read — or watched, or listened to — about this subject that genuinely surprised you or shifted how you see something?
lookingFor: Recent, specific intellectual engagement — not a set text, something the applicant found themselves.

bullets: Name the source with enough detail to be credible
Describe the specific idea or finding that surprised you
Show what it changed — even if it just opened a question
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I listened to a podcast episode featuring a legal historian who argued that most people misread the intent behind landmark civil liberties cases — what we treat as timeless protections were often quite narrow rulings that later courts stretched. That surprised me because I'd been treating case law as progressive and cumulative, and she was making the case that it's far messier and more contingent than that. It made me want to go back and actually read the original judgments rather than the textbook summaries.

commonMistakes: Naming something that's clearly part of the A-level or IB syllabus
Describing something from years ago — the question implies recent engagement
Summarising without saying what it changed or opened up
followUps: Did you go and find out more, or has it stayed as an open question?

question: What part of this course's curriculum are you least excited about, and why?
lookingFor: Honest course knowledge combined with intellectual maturity — the applicant can sit with parts of a subject they don't love.

bullets: Name a specific module or area, showing you've actually read the curriculum
Give a genuine reason — boredom, prior difficulty, mismatch with goals
Show you'll still engage with it properly and explain why
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'll be honest: the quantitative research methods modules in the first year look like the part I'll find hardest to love. Statistics has never been where my instinct lives — I'm stronger with qualitative analysis. But I also know that's exactly the gap I need to close, because the research I care about in the long run relies on being able to read the quant work critically. I'll engage with it seriously; I just know it'll take more deliberate effort than the other units.

commonMistakes: Claiming to be excited about every single module — not credible
Naming a module that's so central it raises doubts about the choice of course
Showing no plan for engaging with the material you find difficult
followUps: Have you done anything to get ahead of that gap before arriving?

question: Is there a specific problem in the world that this subject is your way of addressing? Or is the interest more in the subject itself?
lookingFor: Clarity about whether the motivation is intrinsic (love the subject) or instrumental (solve something) — both are valid, but the applicant should know which.

bullets: Be honest about where your motivation actually sits — don't perform whichever sounds better
If problem-driven, name the problem with specificity
If subject-driven, explain what's compelling about the subject itself
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It's genuinely both, but with a lean toward the problem. I find the maths beautiful in its own right — I'd read about it even if I had no application in mind. But what gets me out of bed is antibiotic resistance: the rate at which we're running out of treatments that work, and the modelling that might help us allocate the ones we have more wisely. The subject is how I get to that problem with the right tools. If I'm honest, I wouldn't be choosing this over other things I love unless the problem was there pulling me.

commonMistakes: Claiming a purely noble problem-driven motivation that sounds performed
Claiming pure intellectual interest when you've mentioned a clear application all along
Being so vague about the problem that it sounds generic
followUps: If that problem were solved tomorrow, what would you study instead?

question: Every subject has stretches that are just hard or dull — not glamorous, just work. How do you know you'll push through those at university, when nobody's chasing you?
lookingFor: Self-knowledge about how the applicant actually sustains motivation — not clichés, but honest strategies.

bullets: Acknowledge the reality — don't pretend it won't happen
Describe a specific strategy or habit grounded in experience
Show awareness that university autonomy is different from school
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've had enough unglamorous stretches in my extended project to know what I'm like when the work is just grind. What's worked for me is keeping a running note of the actual question I'm trying to answer — not the task, but the question underneath it. When I'm stuck in the boring middle of something, rereading that note reminds me why the boring bit matters. I also know I need some external structure even when it's optional, which is why I plan to find a study group early rather than assume self-discipline is enough.

commonMistakes: 'I'm just passionate so I don't get bored' — not credible
Describing strategies with no connection to actual experience
Not acknowledging the difference between school accountability and university independence
followUps: Has there been a stretch in your studies so far where motivation genuinely ran out? What happened?

subtopic: What you expect to be challenged by
question: What do you think will challenge you most about this course specifically — not difficulty in general, but something about how this programme works that doesn't naturally suit you?
lookingFor: Specific self-knowledge matched against genuine course knowledge — the applicant has read the course and thought honestly about the mismatch.

bullets: Name something concrete about the course structure, pedagogy, or expectations
Connect it honestly to a real tendency or limitation in yourself
Show a plan or at least a disposition to meet it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This programme is much more seminar-driven than my school experience, where most of the best work happened on paper. I'm a slower, more deliberate thinker, and I do my best reasoning in writing, not in real-time discussion. The weekly seminars where you're expected to make your argument in the room are going to be uncomfortable for me. I'm not under any illusion that I'll just adapt automatically — I'm planning to over-prepare on the reading specifically so that I have enough to say even when my instinct is to hold back.

commonMistakes: Naming a challenge so generic it says nothing ('it will be harder than A-level')
Showing no awareness of how the course actually works
Being so negative about the challenge that it raises questions about fit
followUps: Have you tried to build that capacity at all before you arrive?

question: You've presumably applied to other universities. How does your interest in this course compare to the others — are you applying here because it's the best fit, or because it's the best option you expect to get?
lookingFor: Honest, intelligent prioritisation — and genuine enthusiasm for this institution, not just its ranking.

bullets: Be honest that you've applied to more than one place — that's normal and expected
Articulate what's genuinely distinctive about this programme relative to alternatives
Avoid being sycophantic — specific reasons beat flattery
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've applied to four programmes and I'd be lying if I said there's a clear winner in every dimension. What makes this one distinct isn't the ranking — two of my other choices sit higher — it's the studio-based assessment model, which none of the others use past first year. I learn by making things and getting critique, not by sitting exams, so that structure is genuinely more suited to how I work. If I get multiple offers, this is the one I'd take. I can say that clearly.

commonMistakes: Pretending this is the only programme you applied to
Ranking it highest without being able to say why specifically
Being so diplomatic that you say nothing useful
followUps: What would make you choose one of the others instead?

question: What will you do if you get here and the subject turns out to be significantly different from what you imagined — harder, less interesting in practice, or not what you expected?
lookingFor: Psychological realism and adaptability — the applicant has thought about disillusionment, not just success.

bullets: Take the scenario seriously rather than dismissing it
Describe an actual response, not just 'I'd push through'
Show you've already done some of the work to calibrate expectations
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've thought about this because I've seen it happen to people I know who chose courses based on a romanticised version of a subject. My honest answer is: I'd give it time — probably a full year — before concluding it wasn't what I expected, because first-year disillusionment is often about the foundations being unsexy, not about the field being wrong. If after a year the gap was real and serious, I'd talk to my tutor about whether I was in the right programme rather than silently struggling. What I wouldn't do is drop out without that conversation.

commonMistakes: Confidently insisting it won't happen — sounds naive
'I'd just push through' with no thought about what that actually means
Immediately jumping to switching courses as the first response
followUps: Has your image of the subject already been adjusted by something you've found out?

question: If you had to describe your interest in this subject as a question — the one you most want your degree to help you answer — what would that question be?
lookingFor: A genuine, original intellectual preoccupation — shows the applicant is driven by curiosity, not just credentials.

bullets: Articulate a specific, genuine question — not a field, not a goal, an actual question
Show why that question matters and where it came from
Be comfortable with the fact that university might complicate rather than answer it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The question I keep circling is: how much of what we call economic development is actually reversible? We talk about growth as if it has a direction, but I keep finding examples where places that were considered 'developed' in certain ways have gone backwards, and the models don't account for that well. I don't expect the degree to give me a clean answer, and I suspect by the third year I'll have a better question rather than an answer. But that's the thread I'm pulling on.

commonMistakes: Answering with a topic or theme rather than an actual question
Picking a question so broad it's effectively not a question
Framing a career goal as an intellectual question
followUps: What do you think you currently don't know that you need to understand it better?

subtopic: Relationship with failure in the subject
question: Tell us about a time you genuinely didn't understand something in this subject — not a gap you quickly fixed, but a real block. How did you get through it?
lookingFor: A real struggle and a genuine process of resolution — shows the applicant knows how to learn when learning is hard.

bullets: Name a specific concept or problem that blocked you, with enough detail to be credible
Describe the actual process — what you tried, in what order, what failed first
Show what finally worked and what it felt like
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Formal proof by induction clicked for everyone around me in a matter of days and I was still lost after two weeks. I understood the mechanics but I couldn't feel why it worked — it seemed circular. I tried three different textbook explanations and they all said the same thing in slightly different words. What finally worked was a video where someone built the intuition using dominoes physically falling — suddenly I could see the structure rather than follow the steps. I learned that when I'm blocked, I need a different representation, not a better explanation of the same one.

commonMistakes: Picking something too easy — 'I didn't understand it at first but figured it out in an hour'
Glossing over what you actually tried — 'I worked harder' isn't a process
Showing no transfer — what did this teach you about how you learn?
followUps: Does that experience change how you plan to approach difficult material at university?

question: How will you know, at the end of this degree, whether it was worth it — and by whose standard?
lookingFor: A personal, considered definition of success that goes beyond grades — shows the applicant thinks about learning as more than performance.

bullets: Define success in terms that include but go beyond the degree classification
Be honest about whose standards matter to you and why
Show that you've thought about what the degree is actually for
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'll know it was worth it if I can read academic work in the field and form a genuine view on it — not just summarise it, but actually argue with it. By the end, I want to be the kind of thinker who can sit with a complex paper and know whether the conclusion follows from the evidence. A good grade is part of that signal, but it's not the whole thing. My standard is mostly my own: can I do the thing I came here to learn to do? I'd know fairly quickly if the answer was no.

commonMistakes: Defining success purely by grade — suggests you've missed what university is for
Defining success by career outcome — might not be in your control by graduation
Being so philosophical that you give no concrete marker at all
followUps: What's the single most important thing you want to be able to do that you can't do now?

subtopic: Disagreeing with consensus in the field
question: Is there a view that's fairly mainstream in this field that you're genuinely not convinced by? What makes you sceptical?
lookingFor: Independent, evidence-grounded critical thinking — not contrarianism, but honest engagement with ideas.

bullets: Name a real position in the field, not a caricature or strawman
Articulate the scepticism with evidence or reasoning, not just instinct
Show appropriate humility — you might be wrong, and you know it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm genuinely sceptical of the strong version of the 'learning styles' claim in education — the idea that students have fixed visual, auditory, or kinesthetic modes that teachers should match. The evidence base seems much weaker than how confidently it's been applied in practice, and most of the studies supporting it have serious methodological problems. I hold that view carefully because I know the research landscape could correct me, but so far the sceptical reading seems more honest. I'm curious what the department here makes of the evidence.

commonMistakes: Picking a genuinely fringe or debunked view and presenting scepticism of it as brave
Scepticism with no reasoning — 'I just feel like it's not right'
Being so hedged that you don't actually commit to a view
followUps: What evidence would change your mind?

subtopic: The gap between school and university
question: What's the biggest difference you expect between studying this subject at school and at university — and are you actually prepared for it?
lookingFor: A realistic understanding of the pedagogical shift plus honest self-assessment of readiness.

bullets: Identify a specific, real difference — not just 'harder' or 'more work'
Be honest about whether you're fully prepared, not just confident
Show you've done something to close the gap rather than just expecting to adapt
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The biggest shift for me will be the expectation to form and defend my own position, not just demonstrate I've understood the material. School assessment mostly rewards accurate replication and structured argument within set parameters. University seminars seem to expect genuine opinion, which I find harder because I naturally want more time and information before committing. I'm not fully prepared for the speed of that, but I've been deliberately practising — taking a position in discussions earlier than feels comfortable and then defending it — because I know I won't develop it just by arriving.

commonMistakes: 'It will just be more intense' — says nothing specific
Claiming to be completely prepared — reads as either naive or dishonest
Identifying the difference but no action taken to prepare for it
followUps: Is there a way you're preparing that hasn't worked? What did you try?

question: If someone senior in your field argued against something you believe quite firmly, what would it take for you to hold your position rather than defer?
lookingFor: The ability to distinguish appropriate deference from intellectual capitulation — principled thinking under social pressure.

bullets: Acknowledge the value of expertise and the reality of social pressure to defer
Articulate the conditions under which you'd hold your position
Show this is grounded in reasoning, not stubbornness
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd take the disagreement seriously — a senior figure has usually thought about the question longer than I have, and I'd want to genuinely understand their argument before responding. But if after engaging with it I thought they were wrong about the evidence, or that their conclusion didn't follow, I'd say so. The social discomfort of disagreeing with someone more senior is real, but I think collapsing your view under that pressure is a different thing from actually changing your mind. I'd want to be able to tell the difference in the moment, which is harder than it sounds.

commonMistakes: Saying you'd always defer to the expert — that's not intellectual engagement, it's abdication
Saying you'd always hold your position — that's stubbornness, not independence
Being so theoretical the answer has no texture — give a hint of the actual feeling of that moment
followUps: Can you think of a time you changed your position because of a good argument rather than because of who was making it?

subtopic: The version of you that might drop out
question: Under what circumstances could you imagine leaving this course — and what would you do if that scenario started to develop?
lookingFor: Honest self-knowledge about the conditions under which motivation fails — and a constructive, non-defensive response.

bullets: Name a real scenario, not just 'I'd never leave' — that's not credible
Show you've thought about early warning signs, not just the endpoint
Describe a proactive response that involves people and support, not just willpower
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: If I found the course bore no relationship to the problem I care about — if everything was theoretical with no path toward application — I could see that eroding my commitment over time. The version of me most at risk of leaving would be the one who stayed silent about that gap for too long, assuming it would resolve itself. If I started feeling that drift, I'd want to name it early — to a tutor, to other students — rather than wait until I'd already disengaged. I think the risk is in the silence more than in the problem itself.

commonMistakes: Insisting it would never happen — not self-aware and not credible
Describing such extreme conditions that they'd never realistically occur
Showing no proactive response — just passive suffering
followUps: Have you spoken to anyone who did leave a degree? What do you take from that?

question: Universities aren't just places you receive an education — they're communities you join and shape. What would you actually add to this department, not just take from it?
lookingFor: A specific, credible sense of what the applicant brings — perspective, experience, intellectual habits — that enriches the department.

bullets: Name something specific and real, rooted in genuine experience or perspective
Frame it in terms of what the department and other students get, not just your own credentials
Avoid generic virtues — 'enthusiasm' and 'hard work' add nothing distinctive
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've spent two years coordinating a community journalism project aimed at people who don't normally engage with local news, which means I've had to think seriously about how ideas reach people who haven't already bought in. That experience of translating complicated things for sceptical audiences is not something most applicants in this department will have, and I think it's relevant beyond my own work — I'd bring it into how I give feedback in seminars and how I approach the public engagement parts of the programme. I also tend to ask practical 'so what' questions that can be disruptive but tend to focus a discussion.

commonMistakes: Listing personal achievements without connecting them to what others gain
Being so modest that you say you have nothing distinctive to offer
Mixing up what you want to gain from the department with what you'll contribute to it
followUps: Is there a gap you've noticed in the department — something they seem not to do, that you'd want to bring?
